_comment: Kitsoki concept pitch. Leads with control inversion and the proofs a coding-agent skeptic can't dismiss: the runtime overrules the model mid-call, edits hot-reload and replay byte-for-byte as tests, and the workflow runs where work already lives. After the one-prompt strawman it credits the Cherny loop (loop engineering: a gated, budgeted agent loop that converges) and argues phases-not-prompts is that idea generalized into a composable graph. The phases arc then shows the gates routing review failures back to implement, plan, or reproduce — and that this can loop multiple times.

theme.name: rose-pine-moon

scenes.subtitleHtml: A conversational workflow engine built for reliable deliveryDeterministically verified workflows. Evidence-first execution. Reliable handoff.

scenes.body: Kitsoki (kit-soh-kee) is a Hopi word for a contemporary settlement — a collection of houses, ceremonial chambers, and public plazas arranged into one living whole.
scenes.lede: The metaphor fits a conversational workflow engine that hosts many surfaces — TUI, daemon, web, GitHub — as connected rooms under one architecture. The name is a small acknowledgment of the serious pre-Columbian astronomy and architecture the Chacoan ancestors of today’s Pueblo peoples encoded into the built landscape.
scenes.narration: FILLER narration — the name Kitsoki is a Hopi word for a settlement of connected rooms under one architecture.

scenes._comment: Generated by scripts/build-loc-growth-slide.py from git tree snapshots — do not hand-edit.
scenes.title: 1060k lines in 11 weeks
scenes.caption: Stacked lines of code by area — the top edge is the whole repo. Engine, tests, stories, and the web UI each carry a real share; nothing hides in an “other” bucket.
scenes.narration: In 11 weeks Kitsoki grew to about 1060 thousand lines — engine, tests, stories, and the web UI each a named, visible share of the whole.

scenes._comment: Generated by scripts/build-loc-growth-slide.py from git tree snapshots — do not hand-edit.
scenes.title: Lines added per week
scenes.caption: Weekly net change by area. Tests rise with engine every week; dips are deliberate removals (e.g. decoupling), not stalls.
scenes.narration: Every week moves across all areas at once, with tests tracking the engine rather than trailing it.

scenes.body: It started with a simple question: can an agent fix a bug end to end? Can we get to 80/20 Autonomy?
scenes.lede: It sounded simple and self-contained - so we chased autonomous bug fixing as the headline capability: take a report, reproduce, fix, verify, ship — no human in the loop.
scenes.narration: FILLER narration — the journey began with autonomous bug fixing.

scenes.body: The fix is the easy part! Everything upstream decides whether it can happen at all.
scenes.lede: Success rate is a lagging indicator downstream of well-defined visual and behavioral expectations, good testing harnesses, good documentation, the ability to capture the regression, and the quality of the bug report itself.
scenes.narration: FILLER narration — autonomous fix success is the outcome of effective upstream processes.

scenes.title: Naïve Success Factors

cells: Clear report, reproducible, expectations already explicit
Many rounds plus access to internal docs
Visual/behavioral expectations, unclear navigation — a serious challenge
Ticket was incomplete — no amount of effort downstream helps

scenes.caption: Only one in eight bugs was solvable as written. The rest were gated upstream — by docs, by UI expectations, and above all by incomplete reports.
scenes.narration: Of eight bugs, only one was solvable as-is. One more took many rounds and internal docs, a quarter were hard UI issues, and half were never solvable because the ticket was incomplete.

scenes.body: Improving bug fixing in a vacuum isn't beneficial.
scenes.lede: It became clear that the overall SDLC quality is what drives bug fix efficiently - without good quality prompts, tools, and especially inputs - there's little to no chance to achieve 80% autonomous operation.
scenes.narration: FILLER narration — push the pain forward into the upstream process.

fields.step1: I'll write a prompt to solve the bug
fields.step2: I'll write a script that does setup and checks the output
fields.step3: I'll write a 4-phase loop-driven pipeline in Python
fields.step4: Now I have 14 phases and 20 Python scripts

scenes.narration: You start with a prompt. Then a script to set it up and check the output. Then a loop-driven pipeline. Before long you're maintaining fourteen phases and twenty Python scripts — full clown makeup.

scenes.body: Processes are a complex adaptive system.
scenes.lede: People, agents, tools, artifacts, failures, and feedback loops keep perturbing each other.
scenes.narration: The Software Development Lifecycle is a complex adaptive system: people, agents, tools, artifacts, failures, and feedback loops.

scenes.title: Root 1: General System Theory

books.title: General System Theory
books.subtitle: Foundations, Development, Applications
books.takeaway: Open systems exchange matter, energy, and information with an environment.

scenes.caption: Systems are structural: behavior lives in the organization of the whole and how the parts interact, the whole is more than the sum of the parts.
scenes.narration: General System Theory frames systems as open, organized, and environment-coupled.

scenes.lede: Processes are built around analagous, systemic patterns.  The ability of that process to effectively interact with the environment to which it is connected drives its capability.
scenes.narration: Kitsoki makes the boundary explicit: state in, artifacts out, feedback always attached.

scenes.title: Root 2: Design in Nature

books.title: Design in Nature
books.subtitle: How the Constructal Law Governs Evolution
books.authors: Adrian Bejan and J. Peder Zane
books.takeaway: Persistent systems evolve structures that make flow easier.

scenes.caption: The constructal law says durable systems change shape to improve access to the currents moving through them.
scenes.narration: Constructal law adds the second root: systems endure by improving flow architecture.

scenes.lede: Structured stories provide the channels that accelerate flow and can be reshaped over time - the flow media is product capability and user feedback.
scenes.narration: Kitsoki makes flow visible so repeated friction can be removed instead of hidden.

scenes.title: Root 3: The Tree of Knowledge

books.title: The Tree of Knowledge
books.subtitle: The Biological Roots of Human Understanding
books.authors: Humberto R. Maturana and Francisco J. Varela
books.takeaway: Knowing is enacted through a self-maintaining loop between agent and environment.

scenes.caption: Maturana and Varela treat cognition as embodied action: a living system keeps itself going by coupling to its world. The observer is part of the system - this feedback between environment and organism or living system is the dynamic of life itself.
scenes.narration: The Tree of Knowledge adds the third root: cognition is a self-maintaining loop.

scenes.lede: The agent, artifact, and environment keep shaping each other over time, so the runtime and framework provide the structure to preserve continuity.
scenes.narration: Kitsoki preserves the loop so work can be inspected, replayed, tested, and improved.

scenes.title: Continuous improvement needs a process
scenes.caption: Lean and Six Sigma work because the process is explicit enough to observe, measure, change, and hold stable.
scenes.narration: Continuous improvement needs a process. Lean and Six Sigma work because the process is explicit enough to measure, change, and hold stable.

scenes.title: Prompts need a process
scenes.caption: A prompt can nudge a model. An explicit workflow captures decisions, grades them, and turns repeated judgment into story changes.
scenes.narration: Prompts can nudge model behavior, but they do not make the system observable. Kitsoki puts the process outside the model: phases, gates, traces, and story diffs.

scenes.body: You have a workflow. People drive it by chatting where the work already lives.
scenes.lede: A GitHub issue. A Jira ticket. A Slack thread. A CRM record.
scenes.narration: You have a workflow. People drive it by chatting where the work lives.

scenes.title: One workflow, many surfaces
scenes.caption: One YAML state machine drives every surface — the same flow runs in a terminal and in a real ticket thread.
scenes.narration: One YAML state machine drives every surface. GitHub, Jira, Slack, or your terminal.

scenes.body: Building this chatbot is harder than it looks.
scenes.lede: Pure LLM agents drift, can't be tested, and break unpredictably. Hand-coded state machines can't parse free text or evolve.
scenes.narration: Building this is harder than it looks. Agents drift. State machines can't parse text.

scenes.title: Cat wrangling
scenes.narration: Orchestrating agents without structure is cat wrangling. Kitsoki ends the wrangling by keeping the runtime in charge.

panels.label: Without structure
panels.caption: The work is improvised in the moment, so drift only shows up after the damage is done.

panels.label: With Kitsoki
panels.caption: The runtime keeps the plan explicit, the work bounded, and the feedback attached.

scenes.caption: Kitsoki removes the wrangling by putting the runtime between intent and action.

scenes.title: Control inversion

panels.label: Typical agent
panels.caption: LLM holds the plan; runtime executes.

panels.label: Kitsoki
panels.caption: Runtime holds the plan; LLM is called as a sub-task.

scenes.caption: The arrow that matters is the LLM's return arrow — a typed intent, not a free-form action.
scenes.narration: Kitsoki flips control. The runtime is in charge. The LLM is called only when interpretation is needed.

scenes.title: One prompt
scenes.caption: One agent. One prompt. No structure between the steps.
scenes.narration: The naive approach: one big prompt. Validate, fix, verify. The agent does everything.

scenes.title: The Cherny loop

nodes.label: goal
nodes.sub: + a gate that can prove it

nodes.label: done
nodes.sub: goal met — or budget ceiling hit

scenes.caption: Loop engineering, after Boris Cherny: an agent iterates toward a goal until a gate proves it's actually met — or a budget ceiling stops it. Launch it once; it converges on its own.
scenes.narration: A Cherny loop gives an agent a goal, checker, and budget. Each failed gate feeds the next try.

scenes.body: Kitsoki the conclusion of this idea — a graph of these loops.
scenes.lede: A Cherny loop solves one goal with one gate and one budget. Kitsoki turns each phase into a separate gated loop, then composes those loops into a durable workflow that can be reused and improved.
scenes.narration: Kitsoki generalizes that loop: every phase gets a gate and budget, then composes into a graph.

scenes._comment: Scene 7–13: full graph shown throughout; one item highlighted per slide.
scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Phase 1: read-only — confirm the bug exists before any code changes.
scenes.narration: Four phases, three gates — starting with reproduce.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Deterministic gate: the test must fail first — no planning until the bug is proven reproducible. A script decides, not a judgment.
scenes.narration: The first gate: test must fail before planning begins.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Phase 2: propose the fix approach — requires operator sign-off before proceeding.
scenes.narration: Plan proposes the fix — awaiting operator sign-off.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Gate: no code written until the plan is approved.
scenes.narration: Gate: no code changes until the plan is signed off.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Phase 3: writes to an isolated worktree — scoped to code changes only.
scenes.narration: Implement writes to a scoped worktree — isolated from main.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Deterministic gate: the tests must pass — review only sees working code. A script decides, for free.
scenes.narration: Gate: tests must pass before sending to human review.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Layer 1 is mechanical: the gate just runs the tests. They fail — or won't even run against the harness — and implement retries itself, in place. No human, no step backward.
scenes.narration: Tests fail? Implement loops in place and fixes the code.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: The last gate is different: review is an agent — an adversarial judgment, not a script. It can approve the work, or send it back.
scenes.narration: Agent review can approve or send the work back.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Three deterministic gates (orange) are scripts — exact, free, incorruptible. The last gate is an agent (violet): an adversarial review for what no test can encode. Pass, and it merges.
scenes.narration: Most gates are deterministic scripts. The final gate is agent review for quality no test can encode.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Just a missed case — back to implement. The plan and root cause stand.
scenes.narration: Missed edge case: back to implement. Same plan, same diagnosis.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: The fix was wrong — back to plan. Re-plan against what review learned.
scenes.narration: Wrong plan: back to plan and choose another approach.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: The root cause was wrong — back to reproduce. Investigate before planning again.
scenes.narration: Wrong root cause: back to reproduce before planning again.

scenes._comment: P1 — the host rejects the model mid-call. The single hardest beat for a coding-agent skeptic to dismiss: their model is its own authority.
scenes.title: The runtime overrules the model
scenes.caption: Even when the LLM is called, the runtime validates the answer — and can reject and re-prompt it mid-call.
scenes.narration: When the LLM is called, the runtime polices it. Off-contract decisions are rejected; valid ones hit the trace.

scenes.title: Permissions are policy, not vibes
scenes.alt: Meme about an agent ignoring Claude Code permissions
scenes.caption: The runtime has to enforce the boundary. If permissions are only a prompt, the agent can negotiate with them.
scenes.narration: This is the permissions version of the same point. Policy belongs in the runtime boundary, not in model vibes.
Prompt engineering. Then loop engineering. Then context engineering. And finally — Kitsoki.

scenes.lede: Edit the YAML and the running machine hot-reloads. Each turn replays byte-for-byte without an LLM call — the spec itself is the regression test. Attach it to GitHub Issues or Jira with one config change.
scenes.narration: Author locally. Edits hot-reload, turns replay without the LLM, then attach GitHub or Jira.

scenes.title: Driven from where work lives

messages.author: alice

messages.body:
Plan updated:
• added negative test for service-account JWT
• tightened middleware match

messages.body:
Acknowledged. Pushing fix…

✓ pushed  a3f8d2c · refactor(auth): use ternary in service-account check

scenes.caption: No local checkout. No "kitsoki run". The state machine runs in CI; comments are the surface.
scenes.narration: Developers drive workflows from where the work lives. Comment on a ticket to refine. Comment on a pull request to push a fix.

scenes.lede: Kitsoki first profiles the repo, asks the operator to review the discovered setup, writes a local project instance, installs the toolkit, and leaves final checks explicit before any expensive work begins.
scenes.narration: Customer-facing usage starts with Kitsoki project onboarding. The flow discovers the repo, reviews the profile, applies local setup files, installs the toolkit, and then runs explicit checks before live work starts.

scenes.title: Kitsoki project onboarding
scenes.caption: The story is linear: discover without writing, review before writing, set up the local Kitsoki instance, then run final checks before real work.
scenes.narration: The actual onboarding story is linear: start, discover without writing, review the profile, set up the local Kitsoki instance, then run final checks before real work starts.

scenes.title: kitsoki run
scenes.subtitle: v1.2.0 · local setup story
scenes.status: session sess_42... · story onboarding · state intro

menuItems.label: Start onboarding
menuItems.hint: discover project, then ask before writes

scenes.footer: [↑/↓ move • Enter pick • Tab chat • Esc cancel]
scenes.caption: The TUI first-run moment stays small: one highlighted menu choice, visible keyboard navigation, then the same reviewed onboarding workflow runs.
scenes.narration: The first screen is intentionally simple: arrow-key selection, Enter to start, then Kitsoki runs the same reviewed onboarding story.

scenes.title: What onboarding does today

items.label: Read-only discovery
items.detail: Infers stack, dev/test/build commands, VCS metadata, tracker identity, starter story pack, and transcript evidence before writes.

items.label: Review gate
items.detail: The operator reviews the profile, commands, starter stories, and feedback; accepting the profile is the first write.

items.label: Local apply
items.detail: Writes .kitsoki.yaml, .kitsoki/project-profile.yaml, a thin project story, explicit final-check script, .gitignore runtime block, and toolkit wiring.

items.label: Replay proof
items.detail: A live gears-rust onboarding was converted into a no-LLM flow and cassette, so the discovery/apply path replays through kitsoki test flows.

scenes.caption: The shipped path is a supervised local setup story, not a vague setup prompt.
scenes.narration: Today onboarding is a supervised local setup story. Discovery is deterministic, review gates writes, apply creates the local Kitsoki instance, and replay fixtures prove the discovery and apply path.

scenes.title: What we plan to add

items.label: Web + VS Code first-run
items.detail: Both surfaces can run existing stories, but new-repo onboarding still needs a first-run path instead of assuming setup is done.

items.label: GitHub start path
items.detail: When work starts from a GitHub issue, pull request, or repo, guide the user into the same Kitsoki project setup path.

items.label: Richer project profile
items.detail: Expand the profile with repo-specific conventions, owners, package boundaries, and working agreements after deterministic discovery.

items.label: Proactive repo analysis
items.detail: Identify git patterns, existing issue and PR templates, CI/release habits, and SDLC flow before choosing story routes or starting live work.

scenes.caption: Next: bring onboarding to every first-run surface and add deeper upfront project analysis.
scenes.narration: The gaps are concrete. Bring the same onboarding story to web and VS Code, make GitHub-originated starts lead into the same setup path, enrich the profile from repo conventions, and proactively analyze git patterns, templates, CI, release habits, and SDLC flow.

scenes.title: After onboarding: governed work

items.label: Story route
items.detail: The local project instance can route into enabled Kitsoki stories such as bugfix, PRD/design, implementation, git operations, and PR refinement.

items.label: Trace first
items.detail: Each run keeps structured events, host calls, choices, costs, and artifacts attached to the work instead of losing them in chat history.

items.label: Failure feedback
items.detail: Bad runs become evidence for story changes, new replay fixtures, or clearer onboarding checks.

items.label: Surface coverage
items.detail: TUI onboarding is proven for gears-rust; web, VS Code, and GitHub-originated first-run paths are tracked as explicit surface-matrix gaps.

scenes.caption: Daily work runs as governed Kitsoki stories.
scenes.narration: Once onboarding is done, the repo has a local Kitsoki instance. Daily work still runs through stories, traces, gates, and follow-up artifacts, with surface coverage tracked honestly.

scenes.lede: After onboarding, the repo enters the real dev-story: operators drive work from Kitsoki's TUI, web UI, GitHub, or editor surfaces, while arena and the bakeoff harness score outcomes against replayable evidence.
scenes.narration: Validation now starts after onboarding: drive the work from Kitsoki surfaces, then score proposals, bug fixes, and journey evidence through arena and the bakeoff harness.

scenes.title: Driven from Kitsoki surfaces

items.label: TUI today
items.detail: Project onboarding and daily story work can run directly in the terminal operator surface.

items.label: Web UI sessions
items.detail: The runstatus web UI can serve the story catalog, live sessions, traces, and replay evidence.

items.label: GitHub starts
items.detail: Issue, PR, or repo-originated work should enter the same Kitsoki project setup and story routes instead of a separate handoff path.

items.label: VS Code surface
items.detail: The editor path should embed the same first-run and session experience rather than inventing a separate workflow.

scenes.caption: The customer path is Kitsoki itself: terminal, browser, issue thread, and editor surfaces all lead into the same stories.

scenes.title: The real post-onboarding story

items.label: Workbench root
items.detail: dev-story's landing room is the free-form workbench for everyday engineering work, not a demo-only script.

items.label: Bugfix route
items.detail: Bug work routes into reproduce, plan, implement, test, review, and PR refinement with explicit gates.

items.label: PRD to design
items.detail: The same hub carries idea and PRD work into design, publish, decomposition, or implementation.

items.label: Repair loops
items.detail: Fix-tests, git operations, docs, deploy, incident, and observability rooms keep follow-on work governed.

scenes.caption: Onboarding is the setup step. dev-story is the day-level product path that follows it.

scenes.title: Validation after onboarding
scenes.caption: After onboarding, Kitsoki can run a real bugfix bakeoff through arena: product-driven work becomes scoreable cells and reports.
scenes.narration: The validation path is now explicit: onboard the repo, drive work through the product surface, route through dev-story, then score cells and reports through arena.

scenes.title: Arena is the validation substrate

items.label: JobSpec cells
items.detail: Targets, variants, axes, placement, and check suites enumerate repeatable comparison jobs.

items.label: Bugfix plugin
items.detail: Bugfix cells reuse the proven bakeoff harness and read a completion-state verdict instead of parsing logs.

items.label: Persona checks
items.detail: Journey and UX verdict adapters fold product-surface QA into the same rollup contract.

items.label: Local / VM placement
items.detail: Cells run in containers with local and remote Docker-host placement; VM pools and live polish remain.

scenes.caption: Arena turns validation into a product pipeline: plan cells, run containers, collect completion-state, and roll up evidence.

scenes.title: Bugfix bakeoff after onboarding

items.label: Repo manifest
items.detail: A new project is added by manifest: repo, install, test command, real bug baselines, and hidden oracles.

items.label: No-cost arming
items.detail: The harness proves RED at baseline and GREEN at the real fix before any live model work is run.

items.label: Live cells
items.detail: Paid cells are explicit operator actions, then score against the same hidden oracle and completion-state contract.

items.label: Cheapest pass
items.detail: Candidate ladders can stop each bug at the first model and effort level that reaches solved.

scenes.caption: This is the immediate validation story after onboarding: arm the repo, run bugfix cells, and score real fixes honestly.

scenes.title: Misses feed the product

items.label: Trace
items.detail: Every run carries structured events, host calls, cost, choices, and artifacts for later review.

items.label: Fixture
items.detail: Useful failures become no-LLM flows, cassettes, or hidden-oracle cases before the story is changed.

items.label: Story diff
items.detail: Prompt, gate, route, and runtime changes are reviewed as code, then replayed against the captured failure.

items.label: Rollup
items.detail: Arena and bakeoff reports preserve failed, partial, blocked, and pending cells instead of turning them into marketing green.

scenes.caption: The learning loop is trace, fixture, story diff, and rollup. That is the training data, not another prompt rewrite.

scenes.title: How runs become reusable tests
scenes.caption: A live run is only useful if we can replay it. We turn each real failure into a test case, score future attempts, and use the results to improve the story.
scenes.narration: A live run becomes useful when we can replay it. Each real failure becomes a test case, the harness scores future attempts, and the result improves the story.

scenes.title: The harness contract
scenes.caption: Think we cherry-picked easy bugs and graded ourselves? You can't game it: the baseline must prove RED or the cell is rejected, the oracle is withheld from the maker, and the grade is re-run independently — oracle plus cost.
scenes.narration: Here is why the score isn't marketing. The baseline must prove red, the oracle is withheld from the maker, and the grade is re-run independently.

scenes.body: Spend only after the case is armed.
scenes.lede: The free path plans arena cells, verifies fixtures, preflights candidates, prepares isolated work areas, and validates the repo-bakeoff flow. Live LLM cells are the explicit gated step, not the default test path.
scenes.narration: The important process change is no-cost first. Verify and prepare everything before spending on a live cell.

scenes._comment: Skeptic proof: ONE of the ten bugs shown in full as a receipt, before the 10/10 summary. Real hidden oracle for bug1/gh-4115 (fix a7080261, baseline e3ab3c27) from tools/bugfix-bakeoff/external/projects/gears-rust/oracles/bug1.rs — abridged for legibility.
scenes.title: libs/toolkit/tests/oracle_bug1.rs

annotations.text: Baseline e3ab3c27 returns 100 → RED. Fix a7080261 returns 50 → GREEN.

scenes.caption: One of the ten, in full. The maker only ever sees the ticket — never this test. Baseline e3ab3c27 is RED; fix a7080261 makes it GREEN, re-run by an independent oracle. Don't trust me: git show a7080261.
scenes.narration: One of the ten in full — a red baseline, a withheld oracle the maker never sees, and the real fix that turns it green.

scenes.title: gears-rust marathon
scenes.caption: Ten real gears-rust fixes went through the pipeline and passed independent oracle verification; the corpus now backs repeatable harness checks.
scenes.narration: The gears-rust marathon shipped and independently verified ten out of ten real fixes, then captured the evidence as a reusable corpus.

scenes.title: gears-rust bugs 1-5
scenes.caption: First half of the gears-rust marathon: five pinned real fixes, five independent oracle passes.
scenes.narration: The first half of the gears-rust marathon is traceable to concrete cases, packages, real fix commits, and independent oracle passes.

scenes.title: gears-rust bugs 6-10
scenes.caption: Second half: the same pipeline shape held across five more packages, with no human edits to the fixes.
scenes.narration: The second half kept the same pipeline shape across five more packages, again with independent oracle passes and no human edits to the fixes.

scenes.title: Hard cases made the harness honest
scenes.caption: A vendor who only shows you green is lying. The stress run went two pass, one FAIL, one STALL — and both failures became generic pipeline hardening, not hidden score inflation.
scenes.narration: The hard-case run was useful because it failed honestly. Two passed, one failed, one stalled, and both problems became generic pipeline fixes.

scenes.title: hard-case stress bugs
scenes.caption: The stress bakeoff stayed honest: H1 and H4 became product hardening, not hidden score inflation.
scenes.narration: The hard-case bakeoff is traceable too. Two passed, one failed with the wrong signal, and one stalled because the runner lacked a watchdog.

scenes.title: query-string live bakeoff
scenes.caption: The query-string run is small but complete: three external parser bugs, three hidden-oracle passes, full suite green each time.
scenes.narration: For query-string, the GPT five point five Kitsoki run solved three out of three cells, with oracle and suite passing each time.

scenes.title: query-string bug traceability
scenes.caption: Each query-string cell starts from the real fix parent and is graded by the real regression test kept out of the maker tree.
scenes.narration: The query-string bakeoff is also traceable: three real filed bugs or pull requests, three pinned baselines, and hidden regression-test grading.

scenes.title: Failure modes became product features
scenes.caption: The harness is built from scars: every recurring failure mode became a deterministic check, a runtime rule, or a story prompt change.
scenes.narration: The harness is not theory. It is a list of failure modes we turned into product behavior.

scenes.title: Persona-driven journey QA in detail

cells: Can one developer complete a realistic journey from evidence?
Covered for core paths; expanding to all flows
In progress: some heuristics are still manual
Can stalled steps resume from trace context?
Partially delivered; recovery KPI rollout pending
Does quality improve over repeated marathons?
In progress until trend dashboard ships

scenes.caption: Process mapping is by persona and evidence, with open gaps tracked explicitly.
scenes.narration: Persona-driven QA tracks reproducibility and confidence by role: what developers need to execute, what QA needs to trust, what operators need for handoff, and what owners need for trend confidence.

scenes.title: Persona-driven journey QA

items.label: Developer persona
items.detail: journey-level trace capture, proposal checkpoints, and bugfix evidence are being replayed from live runs in one coherent flow.

items.label: QA owner persona
items.detail: skeptical review pass is partially automated; partial coverage still relies on manual judgment for UX evidence and edge-case interpretation.

items.label: Operator persona
items.detail: operator handoff artifacts are consistently surfaced, but multi-day cadence dashboards and alerting are still being rolled out.

items.label: Product owner persona
items.detail: cross-repo persona scoring and trend reporting exist as design intent; remaining work is to wire them into a reusable weekly report.

scenes.caption: Persona-driven QA is now a running process, with partial delivery across roles and explicit gaps tracked in the same evidence lane as product validation.

scenes.body: Move from one repo check to reusable system training.
scenes.lede: At scale, failures become scoreable training cases, stories evolve from traces, and the entire loop becomes a repeatable product capability.
scenes.narration: Large-scale training converts operational evidence into model and workflow improvement across projects.

scenes.title: Operational training loop
scenes.caption: The reusable training example is the whole loop: trace, oracle, outcome, story diff, and replayable proof.
scenes.narration: Kitsoki training is operational. The reusable example is trace, oracle, outcome, story diff, and replayable proof.

scenes.title: What the evidence says

items.label: gears-rust marathon
items.detail: ten real merged-fix baselines re-solved through Kitsoki, each independently verified by hidden PR oracles

items.label: query-string bakeoff
items.detail: three external parser bugs solved by gpt-5.5 through Kitsoki with oracle and suite pass

items.label: arena substrate
items.detail: JobSpec cells, bugfix/persona plugins, completion-state, local or VM placement, and check-type rollups are now in the tree

items.label: methodology
items.detail: RED baseline, hidden oracle, isolated cell, explicit trace or completion-state, score before story hardening

scenes.caption: The proof is credible because misses are preserved as product findings, not edited out of the score.

scenes.title: What becomes possible

items.label: Project profile
items.detail: onboarding gives each repo a reviewed profile, command map, story pack, route policy, and validation plan

items.label: Model ladders
items.detail: run cheap-to-expensive candidates through bakeoff or arena specs and stop once a hidden oracle reaches solved

items.label: Story training
items.detail: a failed run produces trace evidence, a story diff, and replay coverage for the next run

items.label: Customer proof
items.detail: drive Kitsoki from the product surface, then answer: did it fix my repo, with my tests, at this cost?

scenes.caption: This is the customer story: Kitsoki is not a smarter prompt. It is a measurable improvement loop around real work.

scenes.title: High leverage tools

items.label: Frontier reasoning
items.detail: spend the strongest model on synthesis, oversight, risk calls, and long-horizon coordination instead of every mechanical step

items.label: Script delegation
items.detail: turn repeated action into commands, tests, renderers, and validators the agent can call instead of re-reasoning

items.label: Model ladders
items.detail: push narrow extraction, classification, and review tasks down to cheaper models when the harness proves they pass

items.label: Structured decomposition
items.detail: rooms, gates, artifacts, and handoffs preserve context across long tasks while keeping action auditable

scenes.caption: Kitsoki is a productivity multiplier for frontier agents: keep their reasoning and oversight, but give them higher-leverage tools than raw tool calls.

scenes.body: The demo becomes an operating system for improvement.
scenes.lede: A customer can onboard a repo with Kitsoki, run governed tasks through the right surface, score real outcomes against hidden oracles, and watch misses turn into story/runtime changes. That is the thing prompts alone do not give them.
scenes.narration: The demo becomes an operating system for improvement: onboard, drive, score, learn, and rerun.

scenes.title: Improves with use

messages.author: kitsoki
messages.body:
Test-review converted from LLM call → deterministic script.

Trace showed 47 identical calls; replaced with a 12-line script.

messages.author: alice
messages.body: feedback for kitsoki — you're being way too verbose in the comments

messages.author: kitsoki
messages.body: Acknowledged. Tuning comment verbosity in next deploy.

messages.author: kitsoki
messages.body:
Indexed your team's internal tooling and docs:
• runbook RUN-042 → embedded in the auth-fix prompt
• stand-validate command → wrapped as a host call

scenes.caption: The trace tells the author where the LLM keeps being asked the same question. Each conversion is one diff against the YAML — and the system absorbs your existing tools as it goes.
scenes.narration: Kitsoki improves with use. It converts repeated LLM calls into scripts. It accepts your feedback. And it indexes your team's existing tools.

scenes.title: Lessons Learned
scenes.narration: Lessons learned.

scenes.title: Demo-driven development
scenes.caption: A proposal is not done when the code compiles. It is done when the product can demonstrate the full behavior and the evidence passes review.
scenes.narration: The development pattern is proposal, implementation, deterministic demo, visual QA, then either fix the gap or ship the proof.

scenes.title: The two-skill loop

items.label: Feature spec
items.detail: the proposal or change summary defines what the viewer must see

items.label: kitsoki-ui-demo
items.detail: records deterministic, no-LLM product video plus frames and chapters

items.label: kitsoki-ui-qa
items.detail: judges the video against scenarios, cites frames, and can downgrade passes

items.label: Iteration
items.detail: a QA failure becomes maker feedback, not a hand-waved demo problem

scenes.caption: The demo and the QA report become part of development, not a marketing pass after development.

scenes.title: What the loop produces

items.label: Product videos
items.detail: feature tours are recorded from the real web UI at watch speed

items.label: Feature pressure
items.detail: if the video cannot show the proposal, the product is not legible enough yet

items.label: Reusable fixtures
items.detail: real traces can become no-LLM flows and host cassettes for repeatable demos

items.label: Site content
items.detail: the same proof assets feed the product site, docs, decks, and release review

scenes.caption: The loop turns feature development into evidence production: working UI, deterministic video, QA report, and public-facing proof.

scenes.title: Next: mockup and storyboard

items.label: Mockup first
items.detail: turn the proposal into concrete screens before the implementation gets expensive

items.label: Storyboard the tour
items.detail: make a video that looks basically like the final product video

items.label: Tune the script
items.detail: refine annotations, narration, and agent instructions against the storyboard

items.label: Build to spec
items.detail: implement the feature once the screen, video, and story behavior are aligned

scenes.caption: Move the first learning loop earlier: proposal to mockup to storyboard to instructions, then implementation. The feature gets built to a visible spec.

scenes.title: Generalized-usage: shipped and live

items.label: Goal-seeker loop
items.detail: the stabilization loop is a kitsoki story: evaluate, dispatch, integrate, re-evaluate, and reject self-reported green.

items.label: Stranger install
items.detail: a downloaded release binary run inside a foreign repo profiles the project, installs the toolkit, and writes local Kitsoki project wiring with no source checkout

items.label: Truthful first run
items.detail: get-started only advertises real actions, and missing providers produce a clear setup message instead of silent replay fallback.

items.label: kitsoki doctor
items.detail: one command reports provider, toolkit, and story-load checks instead of leaving a broken setup to surface mid-session

scenes.caption: These are not roadmap items: each shipped with its own deterministic gate and is live in the current build.

scenes.title: Coming soon: done substrate

items.label: Local model agent
items.detail: builtin.local_llm, managed sidecar, grammar gate, fallback, fetch/cache/inference, and docs are shipped; calibration and live A/B remain

items.label: Offline agent evals
items.detail: kitsoki eval list/show/run, selection metadata, pr-refinement pilot, compact reports, and docs are in place

items.label: Arena comparison runner
items.detail: JobSpec, bugfix/persona plugins, container executor, completion-state rollup, no-LLM default, and local/VM placement are shipped; pool/live polish remain

items.label: Runstatus foundation
items.detail: self-contained artifacts and live trace serving shipped; polish and richer trace modes are the remaining roadmap

scenes.caption: These shipped substrates now support the next product bets.

scenes.title: Coming soon: in progress

items.label: Onboarding surfaces
items.detail: TUI onboarding is proven; web, VS Code, and GitHub-originated first-run paths still need the same project setup flow.

items.label: Proactive repo analysis
items.detail: Expand onboarding to identify git patterns, existing issue and PR templates, CI/release habits, and SDLC flow before live work.

items.label: Arena live product path
items.detail: The no-LLM arena path is real; VM pools, completion-state polling, and one front door for older bakeoff commands remain.

items.label: GitHub + bug reports
items.detail: proposal status is stale, but the current tree shows active work on GitHub-agent and evidence-backed bug reporting surfaces

scenes.caption: These are partially real already: enough exists to prove the direction, but the proposal audit still leaves concrete gaps before each can be called shipped.

scenes.title: Coming soon: next bets

items.label: Capability sandbox
items.detail: unify effect taxonomy, named toolboxes, enforcement, filesystem sandboxing, and trace conformance for every agent kind

items.label: Feedback SDK
items.detail: extract browser feedback capture into a privacy-first framework-neutral SDK with reviewed Slidey reports and a generic sink

items.label: Artifact instances
items.detail: make draft, shared, published, and archived story artifacts resumable, inspectable, and garbage-collectable

items.label: Customer channels
items.detail: add LINE ingress, transport, commerce stories, merchant console, and operator handoff on the existing session engine

scenes.caption: The draft-only queue clusters around a clear product arc: safer agents, portable feedback, artifact-native workflows, and customer-facing channels.

scenes.tagline: Prove what the model did — and what it couldn't
scenes.narration: Kitsoki — prove what the model did, and what it couldn't.
